items.question: k-NN is best described as:
options: Instance-based lazy learning that predicts from k nearest stored training points
Eager parametric learning that fits weights w by gradient descent
Unsupervised clustering into k centroids
A linear model ŷ = Xw + b with closed-form solution
items.answer: Instance-based lazy learning that predicts from k nearest stored training points
items.explanation: k-NN memorizes training data and defers neighbor search to prediction time — no global weight vector is learned.

items.question: For k-NN classification, the default aggregation rule is:
options: Majority vote among k neighbor labels
Minimum neighbor label value
Softmax over neighbor logits
Product of neighbor probabilities
items.answer: Majority vote among k neighbor labels
items.explanation: The predicted class is the label that appears most often among the k closest training examples.

items.question: Increasing k from 1 to a very large value generally:
options: Increases bias and decreases variance of the decision rule
Decreases both bias and variance
Has no effect on model behavior
Eliminates the need for feature scaling
items.answer: Increases bias and decreases variance of the decision rule
items.explanation: Larger k smooths predictions over wider neighborhoods — less flexible boundary, more stable but potentially underfit locally.

items.question: Naive k-NN prediction time per query is:
options: O(n·d) comparing x to all n training points in d dimensions
O(log n) regardless of implementation
O(1) after a one-time training phase
O(k·d) with no dependence on n
items.answer: O(n·d) comparing x to all n training points in d dimensions
items.explanation: Without an index, every query scans the full training set; only after finding distances do you select k smallest.

items.question: Why standardize features before k-NN?
options: So no single high-magnitude feature dominates distance
Because k-NN requires zero mean targets
To convert classification to regression
Because Euclidean distance is undefined on unscaled data
items.answer: So no single high-magnitude feature dominates distance
items.explanation: Distance-based neighbor search is only meaningful when feature scales are comparable — see Scaling and Normalization.

items.question: If k equals the number of training points n, k-NN classification predicts:
options: The global majority class in the training set
The label of the single closest point only
A random class each time
Always the minority class
items.answer: The global majority class in the training set
items.explanation: All training points are neighbors; majority vote equals the most frequent label in the entire dataset.
